entry.title: Washington Post: 22 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Deadly thunderstorm complex cuts power to nearly a million in Canada
  • Russia-Ukraine war live updates: Zelensky says war will end in diplomacy; West discusses atrocities in Hague
  • Who is Anthony Albanese, the new Australian prime minister?
  • Biden signs $40 billion Ukraine aid bill
  • Ukraine’s first lady details war’s toll on the Zelensky family
  • Biden visit tests new South Korean president, a foreign policy novice
  • Inside the collapse of the Rajapaksa dynasty in Sri Lanka
  • In Cuba, a frantic search for milk
  • Russia tries to rebound in Ukraine as prospects for victory fade
  • Russia bans 963 Americans, including Biden and Harris — but not Trump
  • ‘Stocky’ Edwards, Canadian flying ace in World War II, dies at 100
  • S. Korean president appears uneasy when pressed on gender inequality
  • Australia ousts conservative Prime Minister Scott Morrison
  • Gangs strangle Haiti's capital as deaths, kidnappings soar
  • Pakistan's defiant ex-premier calls for march on Islamabad
  • Iran Revolutionary Guard member is shot dead in Tehran
  • Pakistan police arrest 6 men over 'honor killing' of sisters
  • Taliban enforcing face-cover order for female TV anchors
  • Israeli court sentences Palestinian jailbreakers to 5 years
  • Oman drops all remaining coronavirus restrictions
  • Iraqi court postpones smuggling case against Briton, German
  • Pakistani court orders probe into ex-minister's arrest
  • Israel reports first case of monkeypox, suspects others
  • Two years after Floyd’s death, Black Minnesotans say little has changed
  • Biden: U.S. exploring monkeypox vaccines; ‘everybody’ should be concerned
  • From Sandy Hook to Buffalo: Ten years of failure on gun control
  • Live Nation subsidiaries got millions in aid meant for independent venues
  • Davos Meetings Are Full of Potential But Rarely Full of Solutions
  • Do a Third of Americans Truly Believe Replacement Theory?
  • Why Goofy Princeton Reunion Rituals Mean So Much to Me
  • John Fetterman’s Vaguely Encouraging Politics
  • Why Sweden’s Stance on Kurds Riles Turkey’s Erdogan
  • Why Wiping Out Smallpox Has Stoked Risk of Monkeypox
  • Racism holds back all of us, including White people
  • Putin is starving millions of people around the world
  • ‘Long covid’ is going to be a long haul
  • The Post endorses Eleanor Holmes Norton, the District’s first line of defense
  • Ask Eugene Robinson about the latest news around the U.S. and world
  • Chat with Perry Bacon Jr. about politics and beyond
  • Chat with Jennifer Rubin about her columns, politics, policy and more
  • Send a letter to the editor
  • Contact the reader representative
  • Submit an op-ed
Breaking news and analysis on politics, business, world national news, entertainment more. In-depth DC, Virginia, Maryland news coverage including traffic, weather, crime, education, restaurant reviews and more.

The Korea JoongAng Daily is an English-language daily published by the JoongAng Group, Korea’s leading media group, in association with The New York Times.

entry.title: South China Morning Post: 22 May, 2022
entry.summary: South China Morning Post: 22 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-22 14:35PM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Hongkong Post changes pupil uniforms from yellow to white in stamp photo marking city’s 25th handover anniversary
  • Hong Kong customs arrests 3, seizes HK$77 million worth of black-market cigarettes
  • Coronavirus: 3 new cases possibly tied to cluster at quarantine hotel in heart of Hong Kong nightlife district, city logs 237 new cases
  • Urban Renewal Authority to supply 22,000 flats in Hong Kong over next 5 years, costing HK$120 billion, but warns of potential cash crunch
  • Two-time kidney transplant recipient in Hong Kong urges others to consider living organ donations to save lives
  • Hong Kong should resume quarantine-free travel with foreign countries first, chief of leading business chamber in city urges government
  • Hong Kong driver killed in early-morning collision with stationary tour bus
  • How Hong Kong’s next chief executive can show he means business with ‘result-oriented’ reform
  • Canada moves up to No 2 for Hong Kong students going abroad, with easy migration pathways a major draw
  • German carmaker Audi, ad agency M&C Saatchi apologise for copyright infringement in video campaign with Hong Kong’s Andy Lau
  • Why Chinese demand for African imports might defy Covid lockdowns, supply chain snarls
  • China drums up support for global security push in Latin America as US looks to Asia
  • Chinese space scientists aim for telescope to find next Earth up to 32 light years away
  • Australia’s new PM Albanese has chance to mend fences with China, analysts say
  • China pledges US$1.5b in grain farmer subsidies to offset soaring production costs
  • Mali’s rosewood crisis ‘not happening in a vacuum’ – and China is a key buyer
  • Shanghai’s coronavirus cases fall to the lowest since March 20 as city keeps community infections at zero
  • China’s Big Tech sees new wave of job cuts as Covid lockdowns hurt economy
  • Taiwan and Shanghai – 2 different takes on tackling Covid-19
  • China-US relations: as world watches Ukraine, navy missions in the Pacific quietly raise tension
  • ‘Fat’ wedding: Chinese woman told to apologise for comments about photography studio she claimed ruined her bridal photos
  • Ukraine war lessons spur China military shipbuilding surge: experts
  • In Japan, Biden to launch economic plan for region sceptical on benefits
  • Will India’s demand for Malaysia’s palm oil dip after Indonesia export ban reversal?
  • South Korea’s Yoon Suk-yeol to unveil own Indo-Pacific strategy following summit with US President Joe Biden
  • Renowned Indian dancer Arunima Kumar to perform Kuchipudi for Britain’s Queen Elizabeth’s Platinum Jubilee
  • India’s export ban makes wheat cheap – hurting farmers already reeling from extreme heat
  • Australia votes for climate action with rejection of coal-loving PM Scott Morrison
  • As Sri Lanka defaults for the first time, what’s next for the Rajapaksa family?
  • Biden says US ‘ready for anything North Korea does’ as he wraps up Seoul trip with Hyundai’s US$10 billion investment pledge
  • In South Asia, Bangladesh’s ‘deft’ balancing of US, China and India ties stands out
  • Indonesian-Chinese writer Marga T on guilt of having been born a girl: ‘it gave me cancer’
  • Coronavirus: North Korea reports ‘positive trend’ in Covid fight as ‘fever’ cases dip
  • Australia’s next prime minister came from humble beginnings, says ‘reach for the stars’
  • Queen Elizabeth’s Jubilee parade to include corgis, horses, and swans
  • Ukraine rules out ceasefire as fighting intensifies in Donbas
  • Taliban order leads to once defiant Afghan women TV presenters covering their faces on air
  • WHO says ‘urgent need’ for package of measures against monkey pox
  • Ukraine war: fate of 2,500 POWs from Mariupol steel plant stirs concern
  • UK vows ‘cast-iron commitment’ to Northern Ireland after Nancy Pelosi sounds trade alarm
  • ‘Puncture-proof’ airless tyres edge closer to becoming a reality
  • US baby milk formula airlift due to begin with flight from Europe; emergency programme
  • Tornado hits US’ northern Michigan, at least two dead
  • Moscow bans 900 prominent Americans from entering Russia, including Hollywood actor Morgan Freeman
  • Dangerous DIY baby milk formula recipes in US go viral as parents get desperate
  • Coronavirus: US faces ‘dire’ risk of vaccine rationing without more funds
  • Ukraine war: Turkey’s Erdogan talks to Nato hopefuls Sweden and Finland, urges an end to support for ‘terror’ groups
  • Biden’s Indo-Pacific economic plan is yet another example of ‘with us or against us’ oversimplification
  • HSBC CEO rebukes comments on climate change by top banker, says they are ‘inconsistent’ with lender’s strategy
  • Chinese green bond issuers must improve disclosures to ensure growth of sustainable finance market, report says
  • Binance readies licensed cryptocurrency platform in Middle East in a bid to regain legitimacy
  • Climate change: More Chinese and Asian firms disclose greenhouse-gas emissions, but lag in reduction commitments, data quality
  • Cannes 2022: Return to Seoul movie review – adopted from South Korea, raised in France, a woman looks for biological parents in Davy Chou’s nuanced tale about searching for one’s past
  • Asian-Canadian millennials talk about travel during Covid, living through the pandemic and future overseas travel plans
  • What is neem? The health benefits of what Ayurveda calls the world’s most versatile medicinal plant, from boosting immunity to fighting cancer
  • Adeline Ooi, Asia director of Art Basel, on getting into the art fair business and what it’s like to build ‘a floating city for five days’
  • ‘Spectacular’ Louis Vuitton fashion show, attended by Eileen Gu, Gemma Chan and Maude Apatow, proves why digital events can’t compare to in-person experiences
  • Classic Hong Kong cinema: John Woo on action, violence, influences, growing up in poverty and working in Hollywood in 1997 interview
  • Why dating as an Asian-American woman can mean hyper-sexualisation and fetishisation, reducing people to objects
  • 9 best new luxury hotels in Asia: Joali gets its second Maldives spot, The Standard opens in Bangkok, and Regent Hong Kong returns to Tsim Sha Tsui harbourfront
  • Meet Heidi Klum’s teen model daughter, Leni: she’s the face of Dior Beauty and Michael Kors, and even landed a Vogue Germany cover – yet still worked a summer job at an ice cream parlour
  • Inside Paris Hilton’s extravagant luxury car collection, from her ‘Paris-ized’ holographic BMW i8 Roadster and Barbie pink Bentley Continental GT, to the Mercedes-Benz her millionaire ex gave her
  • How does Neymar spend his millions? The Paris Saint-Germain footballer bought Bored Ape NFTs, reportedly flies in a Cessna 680 jet and splurged US$180,000 on 16 GaGá Milano watches and accessories
SCMP.com, Hong Kong's premier online English daily provides exclusive up-to-date news, audio video news, podcasts, RSS Feeds, Blogs, breaking news, top stories, award winning news and analysis on Hong Kong and China.

entry.content:

entry.title: Wired Magazine: 22 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Bill Gates Is So Over This Pandemic
  • Paradise at the Crypto Arcade: Inside the Web3 Revolution
  • Fast, Cheap, and Out of Control: Inside Shein’s Sudden Rise
  • Welcome to Janelle Monáe’s Dreamworld
  • The End of Alcohol
  • Volodymyr Zelensky and the Art of the War Story
  • Can Social Media Be Redeemed?
  • Six-Word Sci-Fi: Stories Written by You
  • Do I Really Need to Back Up All My Data?
  • The Intoxicating Pleasure of Conspiratorial Thinking
  • Time Got So Much Weirder. The World Needs a New Lexicon
  • This Startup Wants to Get in Your Ears and Watch Your Brain
  • A Farmer’s Quest to Beat California’s Waves of Drought and Deluge
  • The Bitcoin Bust That Took Down the Web’s Biggest Child Abuse Site
  • A Million Little Pieces: The Race to Rebuild the World’s Reefs
  • If Someone Is Typing, Then Stops … Can I Ask Why?
  • I Finally Reached Computing Nirvana. What Was It All For?
  • The Enduring Legacy of Clubhouse’s Chatty Revolution
  • Is Russia’s Largest Tech Company Too Big to Fail?
  • It’s Like GPT-3, but for Code—Fun, Fast, and Full of Flaws
  • The Infinite Reach of Joel Kaplan, Facebook’s Man in Washington
  • ‘I’m the Operator’: The Aftermath of a Self-Driving Tragedy
  • Trapped in Silicon Valley’s Hidden Caste System
  • Zillow Botched Buying Homes. Opendoor Thinks It Can Do Better
  • The History—and Disturbing Resurrection—of Black Androids
  • Jacques Vallée Still Doesn’t Know What UFOs Are
  • The Best Way to Learn Online? Be a Lurker
  • Driving While Baked? Inside the High-Tech Quest to Find Out
  • Ada Palmer and the Weird Hand of Progress
  • How Telegram Became the Anti-Facebook
  • Do Good Doorbell Cams Make Good Neighbors?
  • 6 Apps That Take the Grind out of Your Workday
  • They Were ‘Calling to Help.’ Then They Stole Thousands
  • North Korea Hacked Him. So He Took Down Its Internet
  • Here Come the Underdogs of the Robot Olympics
  • Is It OK to Listen to a Butt-Dial Message?
  • This 22-Year-Old Builds Chips in His Parents’ Garage
  • A Grand Unified Theory of Buying Stuff
  • Can Being Reminded of My Death Improve My Life?
  • The Best of CES 2022
  • In Praise of Unglamorous American Invention
  • Covid Will Become Endemic. The World Must Decide What That Means
  • Advice to Startup Founders: Prepare to Fail
  • The Quest to Trap Carbon in Stone—and Beat Climate Change
  • The Morbid War Over Online Obituaries
  • The Wave-Conquering, Metaverse-Crashing Life of Kai Lenny
  • Welcome to Miami, Where All Your Memes Come True!
  • 4 Dead Infants, a Convicted Mother, and a Genetic Mystery
  • The Twitter Wildfire Watcher Who Tracks California’s Blazes
  • The Matrix Is the Best Hacker Movie
  • Welcome to the (Synthetic) Meatspace
  • WIRED Peers Into the Future of Reality
  • The Most Efficient Way to Debug the Simulation
  • What The Matrix Got Wrong About Cities of the Future
  • Yahya Abdul-Mateen II Is Ready to Blow Your Mind
  • It’s Time to Reimagine the Future of Cyberpunk
Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

  • Indicators: Economic data, commodities and markets

Global news and current affairs from a European perspective. Best downloaded on Friday mornings (GMT)

entry.title: The Diplomat: 21 May, 2022
entry.content:

The Diplomat is a current-affairs magazine for the Asia-Pacific, with news and analysis on politics, security, business, technology and life across the region.

entry.title: The Third Pole: 20 May, 2022
entry.summary: The Third Pole: 20 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-20 10:56AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

The Third Pole is a multilingual platform dedicated to promoting information and discussion about the Himalayan watershed and the rivers that originate there.

entry.title: Nature: 19 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • The toll of menopause: how universities can help
  • Nature journals raise the bar on sex and gender reporting in research
  • How climate law can help to prevent the next pandemic
  • The metrics that predict the course of type 2 diabetes
  • Mysterious cosmic thief is stealing a dwarf galaxy’s gas

  • ‘Barcodes’ reveal a cancer’s life story

  • Flower power: ‘Sunflower’ system churns out useful energy

  • Coronavirus ‘ghosts’ found lingering in the gut
  • Origin of life theory involving RNA–protein hybrid gets new support
  • The pandemic’s true health cost: how much of our lives has COVID stolen?
  • How three Ukrainian scientists are surviving Russia’s brutal war
  • Rise of drug-resistant tuberculosis is hidden in plain sight
  • AI can burn or brighten, and intangible economics: Books in brief
  • Want to prevent pandemics? Stop spillovers
  • One statistical analysis must not rule them all
  • Childcare crowdfunding campaigns aim to keep mums on the academic track
  • Immune to frustrations
  • Giant collision created galaxies devoid of dark matter
  • Young cerebrospinal fluid improves memory in old mice
  • A lab-on-a-chip that takes the chip out of the lab
  • Killing the messenger to evade bacterial defences
  • Tension hones body segmentation around the clock
  • A trail of dark-matter-free galaxies from a bullet-dwarf collision
  • Direct observation of the dead-cone effect in quantum chromodynamics
  • Polariton Bose–Einstein condensate from a bound state in the continuum
  • Observation of a molecular bond between ions and Rydberg atoms
  • Microcomb-driven silicon photonic systems
  • Microfluidic chain reaction of structurally programmed capillary flow events
  • Programmable heating and quenching for efficient thermochemical synthesis
  • Intermolecular [2π+2σ]-photocycloaddition enabled by triplet energy transfer
  • Synergistic HNO3–H2SO4–NH3 upper tropospheric particle formation
  • Expanding ocean food production under climate change
  • A natural mutator allele shapes mutation spectrum variation in mice
  • Genetic and chemotherapeutic influences on germline hypermutation
  • Young CSF restores oligodendrogenesis and memory in aged mice via Fgf17
  • Left–right symmetry of zebrafish embryos requires somite surface tension
  • Phage anti-CBASS and anti-Pycsar nucleases subvert bacterial immunity
  • Group A Streptococcus induces GSDMA-dependent pyroptosis in keratinocytes
  • Landscape of helper and regulatory antitumour CD4+ T cells in melanoma
  • Selective inhibition of miRNA processing by a herpesvirus-encoded miRNA
  • Nonlinear mechanics of human mitotic chromosomes
  • Design of protein-binding proteins from the target structure alone
  • Structural basis of NPR1 in activating plant immunity
  • USP14-regulated allostery of the human proteasome by time-resolved cryo-EM
  • Author Correction: The guidance receptor plexin D1 is a mechanosensor in endothelial cells
  • Publisher Correction: Signatures of TOP1 transcription-associated mutagenesis in cancer and germline
  • Addendum: Large metallicity variations in the Galactic interstellar medium
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nauture also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

entry.title: Asian Review of Books: 20 May, 2022
entry.summary: Asian Review of Books: 20 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-20 00:16AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • “The Bangalore Detectives Club” by Harini Nagendra
  • Podcast with Glynne Walley, translator of “Eight Dogs, or ‘Hakkenden’: Part One—An Ill-Considered Jest” by Kyokutei Bakin
  • New Book Announcement: “Stone Masters: Power Encounters in Mainland Southeast Asia”, edited by Holly High
  • “Vernacular English: Reading the Anglophone in Postcolonial India” by Akshya Saxena
  • “Breathless” by Amy McCulloch
  • Eliot and I
  • “Everest 1922: The Epic Story of the First Attempt on the World’s Highest Mountain” by Mick Conefrey
  • New Book Announcement: “Still Lives” by Reshma Ruia
  • “All the Lovers in the Night” by Mieko Kawakami
  • “Letters to Singapore” by Kelly Kaur
In addition to reviewing books about or of relevance to Asia, the Asian Review of Books also features long-format essays by leading Asian writers and thinkers, to providing an unparalleled forum for discussion of key contemporary issues by Asians for Asia and a vehicle of intellectual depth and breadth where leading thinkers can write on the books, arts and ideas of the day. Widely quoted and referenced, with an archive of more than one thousand book reviews, it is the only web resource dedicated to Asian books. And now, with the addition of the new premium content, the Asian Review of Books, is a must-read publication.

entry.title: Five Books: 20 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Alison King on Best Verse Novels for 8-12 Year Olds
  • Sue Rainsford on Literary Horror Books
  • Howard Smith on Antitrust Books
  • Carole Hillenbrand on The Best History Books: the 2022 Wolfson Prize Shortlist
  • Velma Šarić on Bosnia Books
  • Mark Webber on NATO Books
  • Wajahat Ali on The Best South Asian American Novels
  • Breege O'Brien on Great Teen Reads from Ireland’s Great Reads Awards Books
  • Judy Golding on The Best William Golding Books
  • Alison Richard on Madagascar Books
  • Anand Menon on Brexit Books
  • Barbara Band on The Best Baby Books
  • Annebella Pollen on Understanding the Nude Books
  • Gala Sicart on The Best Catalan Fiction
  • Alison Weir on The Best Tudor Historical Fiction
  • Federica Romei on Inflation Books
  • Peter Lawlor on Rock Music Books
  • Chris Power on The Best Literary Thrillers
  • Richard Vinen on Modern French History Books
  • Hanna Komar on Five of the Best Works of Belarusian Literature
  • Jonathan Kwan on The Austro-Hungarian Empire
  • Michele Cobb on The Best Audiobooks: the 2022 Audie Awards
  • Serhii Plokhy on Ukraine and Russia Books
  • Sophie Roell on Notable Nonfiction of Early 2022
  • Cal Flyn on Notable Novels of Spring 2022
  • Marion Turner on Best Medieval Historical Fiction
  • Tanya Selvaratnam on Domestic Violence Books
Expert book recommendations

entry.title: London Review of Books: 26 May, 2022
entry.summary: London Review of Books: 26 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-19 10:16AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • James Meek - How Civil Wars Start – And How to Stop Them
  • Letters
  • Clare Jackson - Elizabeth Stuart: Queen of Hearts
  • James Butler - Short Cuts: Limping to Success
  • Hazel V. Carby - The 1619 Project: A New American Origin Story  / Exterminate All the Brutes
  • Michael Wood - At the Movies: ‘Everything Everywhere All at Once’
  • Rosa Lyster - Empire of Diamonds: Victorian Gems in Imperial Settings  / Blood, Sweat and Earth: The Struggle for Control over the World’s Diamonds
  • Jan-Werner Müller - Germany Inc.
  • A.E. Stallings - Poem: ‘The Sieve’
  • Ferdinand Mount - Promised Lands: The British and the Ottoman Middle East
  • James Romm - Olympia: A Cultural History
  • Ange Mlinko - Rapture and Melancholy: The Diaries of Edna St Vincent Millay
  • Tim Parks - Kapo
  • Hal Foster - At Tate Modern: ‘Surrealism beyond Borders’
  • Freya Johnston - The Life of the Mind
  • Alex Harvey - The N’Gustro Affair  / No Room at the Morgue
  • Anne Enright - On the Sands
  • Francis Gooding - Wild Thought: A New Translation of ‘La Pensée sauvage’
  • Rosemary Hill - Diary: At Mars Avenue
Literary review publishing essay-length book reviews and topical articles on politics, literature, history, philosophy, science and the arts by leading writers and thinkers
